chunk_summary: Iron supplementation in SAM is delayed until the rehabilitation phase to avoid oxidative stress during stabilization. Initiation requires clinical stability, controlled infection, and adherence to dosing guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the rationale for delaying iron supplementation during SAM stabilization.
Identify clinical criteria for initiating iron during the rehabilitation phase.
Learn appropriate dosing and monitoring protocols for iron supplementation in SAM.
Recognize alternative management strategies for severe anemia in SAM.
key_facts: Iron supplementation is delayed until the rehabilitation phase to prevent oxidative stress during stabilization.
Initiate elemental iron at 3 mg/kg/day only after clinical stability, including improved appetite, resolved hypothermia, and controlled infection.
Iron supplementation should be paired with folate and nutrition rehabilitation without escalating doses to force hemoglobin recovery.
Severe anemia (Hb <4 g/dL or Hb 4–6 g/dL with respiratory distress) requires cautious blood transfusion per facility protocol.
Persistent poor weight gain or anemia triggers reassessment for infection, inflammation, parasitosis, or hemoglobinopathy.
reference_quote: Iron supplementation should only begin during the rehabilitation phase when the child is clinically stable and infection is controlled. (WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children)
document_key_topics: Clinical criteria for rehabilitation phase interventions
Management of severe anemia in SAM
Monitoring and reassessment during SAM treatment
